title: Stock picking: Aussie AI trade at risk from overcrowding
full_text:
Australia's lack of exposure to large artificial intelligence stocks is forcing fund managers to pile into a group of smaller so-called picks-and-shovels companies that have generated huge returns, but are becoming overcrowded.
ASX investors have long been envious of Wall Street's outsized exposure to technology stocks at the forefront of the AI revolution from chipmakers Nvidia and Broadcom to the hyperscalers such as Microsoft and Amazon. In contrast, Australia's tech sector makes up just 2 per cent of the local bourse by weighting, and roughly half a per cent of the index's earnings.
At the larger end of the sharemarket, there are few options that offer access to the world's hottest trade, and those that are available have disappointed this year - data centre developers NextDC and Goodman Group are up just 12 per cent and down 2 per cent, respectively.
This means fund managers have been forced to hunt in the smaller end of the ASX for companies powering the expansion of Australia's digital infrastructure.
That is until a flurry of tech companies - including Firmus and Sharon AI - hit the boards later in the year.
"The businesses building the infrastructure behind AI are where we're finding some of the most exciting opportunities locally," said James Barker, a portfolio manager at Ellerston Capital.
"Investors can gain very little exposure to these themes through theS&P/ASX 20. Many of the companies benefiting most from these structural shifts are operating in the micro and small-cap universe."
One of Ellerston's top performers has been SKS Technologies. The firm first bought shares in July 2024 at about $1.30, and it's now trading above $9 thanks to surging demand for hyperscale data centres and AI infrastructure.
Barker also named electrical services stocks Southern Cross Electrical Engineering and GenusPlus, whose orders books are expanding from the growing investment in data centres, power infrastructure and electrical networks.
"The market is still underestimating the duration of this investment cycle," Barker said. "We're seeing project pipelines extending into 2028 and 2029, which gives these businesses far stronger revenue visibility than they had only a few years ago."
Centennial Asset Management also owns Genus Plus and Southern Cross Electrical, alongside technology infrastructure business Megaport and data centre service provider Macquarie Technology Group.
Portfolio manager Matthew Kidman said many of these companies were "nothing businesses" and broadly underowned until the data centre boom came along, which has boosted their valuation enough to appear on the radar of larger fund managers.
"Many of these stocks were just like buds that have all of a sudden bloomed into flowers," he said.
"But since the start of the new financial year, there's been a big rotation out of these contractors because they're becoming crowded. But I think we'll see those companies perform strongly in the August reporting season as they give really solid outlooks."
Since June 30, SKS is down 9 per cent, Southern Cross Electric 10 per cent, GenusPlus 15 per cent and Megaport 6 per cent.
A similar trend occurred in 2025 when fund managers piled into a group of small-cap darlings including Life360, Generational Development, Zip Co and Catapult, only to fall victim to a violent reversal this year.
However, fund managers are confident the AI trade is still sustainable.
Ten Cap's lead portfolio manager Jun Bei Liu said Australia had quietly become a key cog in the global AI supply chain as foreign tech companies search for the land, power, connectivity, construction capability and materials that make AI infrastructure possible.
"Investors always ask me 'why would I want to invest in Australian equities - it's so boring and old school, and we don't have companies like SpaceX and Nvidia'," Liu said. "The chips and frontier models are obviously offshore, and they're the famous names you hear about, but everything that surrounds them - we call the picks and shovels - are, in many cases, underowned."
Liu believes the most underappreciated AI trade for ASX investors is in the resources sector, particularly copper stocks.
The base metal is used across the entire supply chain from grid connection, transmission, transformers and switchboards to internal cabling, cooling infrastructure and back-up power.
"The reason this layer matters is that as AI demand tightens these markets, there isn't enough supply coming online fast enough," Liu said.
"From finding a copper mine to actually digging it out, it now takes 18 years, so that's why major mining companies are out there buying existing mines."
Fund managers are also wary of the wave of tech companies expected to list on the ASX over the next year.
FDC Consolidated - which popped 17 per cent on its market debut this week - is a construction company but has talked up its clients, including data centre operators such as ASX-listed NextDC and US-listed Digital Realty.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Alex Gluyas
heading: Data Science and AI
